coredata.dc:title: Development of folate-conjugated polypyrrole nanoparticles incorporated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for targeted bioimaging and photothermal therapy

coredata.dc:description: PPy nanoparticles are widely employed as PTT agents, because of their exceptional near-infrared absorption properties. Nonetheless, the efficacy of PTT with PPy nanoparticles is hindered by a challenge, specifically, a lack of precise targeting. In this study, a PTT imaging agent was developed by combining NCQDs having bright green fluorescent properties with PPy nanoparticles along with the masking of folic acid to overcome the challenge of targeting. The synthesized PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite, characterized by extraordinary photothermal property, was utilized for imaging of folate receptor positive (FA+) MCF-7 cancer cells through the emission of green fluorescence by NCQDs incorporated within the nanocomposite. Additionally, these nanoparticles demonstrated a good level of cell viability, exceeding 82 %, even at a concentration of 600 μg mL−1. Even the in vivo toxicity inspection of the nanocomposite exemplified no observed acute toxicity at experimental dosages of 1 and 3 mg per kg body weight. By subjecting MCF-7 cells, inoculated with 100 μg mL−1 of nanocomposite, to NIR laser irradiation for 5 min, a significant decline in cell viability was witnessed, establishing the photothermal therapeutic potency of the nanocomposite. The death of cancer cells induced by nanocomposite was verified through MTT assay, imaging of cells by NCQDs alone, with nanocomposite, and by live/dead cell Calcein AM/PI staining assay. Quantification of induced apoptosis post-laser treatment is conducted through staining with Annexin V-FITC/PI. These findings establish potential use of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite as versatile theranostic agents, capable of targeted bioimaging and treatment for cancer cells exhibiting folate receptors.

head.ce:title: Development of folate-conjugated polypyrrole nanoparticles incorporated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for targeted bioimaging and photothermal therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: PPy nanoparticles are widely employed as PTT agents, because of their exceptional near-infrared absorption properties. Nonetheless, the efficacy of PTT with PPy nanoparticles is hindered by a challenge, specifically, a lack of precise targeting. In this study, a PTT imaging agent was developed by combining NCQDs having bright green fluorescent properties with PPy nanoparticles along with the masking of folic acid to overcome the challenge of targeting. The synthesized PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite, characterized by extraordinary photothermal property, was utilized for imaging of folate receptor positive (FA+) MCF-7 cancer cells through the emission of green fluorescence by NCQDs incorporated within the nanocomposite. Additionally, these nanoparticles demonstrated a good level of cell viability, exceeding 82 %, even at a concentration of 600 μg mL−1. Even the in vivo toxicity inspection of the nanocomposite exemplified no observed acute toxicity at experimental dosages of 1 and 3 mg per kg body weight. By subjecting MCF-7 cells, inoculated with 100 μg mL−1 of nanocomposite, to NIR laser irradiation for 5 min, a significant decline in cell viability was witnessed, establishing the photothermal therapeutic potency of the nanocomposite. The death of cancer cells induced by nanocomposite was verified through MTT assay, imaging of cells by NCQDs alone, with nanocomposite, and by live/dead cell Calcein AM/PI staining assay. Quantification of induced apoptosis post-laser treatment is conducted through staining with Annexin V-FITC/PI. These findings establish potential use of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite as versatile theranostic agents, capable of targeted bioimaging and treatment for cancer cells exhibiting folate receptors.

A visual summary representing all aspects of the research conducted.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The integration of bioimaging and curative functionalities into nanoscale platforms has been a promising approach in the field of anti-tumor therapy. These nanoscale platforms, often referred to as theranostic nanocomposites, are designed to diagnose, monitor, and treat cancer simultaneously [1]. The selection of optimal bioimaging and therapeutic nanoparticles is a pivotal step in advancing the development of effective and high-performance nanocomposites in this context. Quantum dots demonstrated fascinating bioimaging applications due to their ultra-small size, good photostability, controllable fluorescence, biocompatibility, and non-toxicity, drawing significant interest in the biomedical field [2–6]. Carbon quantum dots(CQDs) are increasingly replacing semiconductor quantum dots due to several advantages such as biosafety, environmental impact, abundance of raw materials for large-scale synthesis, tunable properties, and overall versatility [7–10]. Among CQDs, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots(NCQDs) are proving their strong potential for bioimaging owing to their improved quantum yield and optical properties resulting from doping [4,11–15]. In the field of cancer therapeutics, the most promising and advanced approach is photothermal therapy (PTT), which involves targeting and killing cancer cells specifically by producing heat (hyperthermia) through the association of light with certain nanoscale particles. These nanoparticles are designed to absorb light in the near-infrared (NIR) region, which is known to be the region of high light penetration through tissues [16]. PTT offers several advantages, including minimal invasiveness, precise spatial and temporal control, and the potential for combination with other therapeutic modalities [17]. Gold nanomaterials, particularly gold nanorods and gold nanoshells, have been extensively researched and are among the most well-established types of photothermal therapy (PTT) nanoagents [18–20]. Along with this, there is a growing interest in exploring the potential of various other inorganic nanoscale particles such as Palladium nano-sheets [21], copper selenide particles [22], copper sulfide nanoparticles [23], magnetic nanoparticles, essentially iron oxide nanoparticles [24,25] and carbon nanomaterials (e.g., nanographene, nanotubes, etc.) [26–29]. However, the presently employed inorganic photothermal nanoagents are often non-biodegradable and can persist within the body for extended durations following systemic administration. Therefore, there is a notable shift in the application of photothermal agents from inorganic to organic materials, primarily driven by the biodegradability and low toxicity associated with organic photothermal agents [30–33]. In recent years, Polypyrrole(PPy), one of the organic conductive polymers, established its ability as a significant photothermal agent in the discipline of photothermal therapy due to its high conductivity, broad range of light absorption capabilities, and stability [34–37]. In photothermal therapy, non-targeted delivery of heat to the tumor area, often associated with conventional hyperthermia treatments, can result in adverse reactions to healthy tissues. This is primarily due to the fact that heat is not selectively confined to the cancerous cells, and surrounding healthy tissues are also exposed to elevated temperatures [38,39]. To address this challenge, researchers are exploring targeted hyperthermia approaches, such as using receptor ligands that can selectively bind to tumor tissues [40–42]. The overexpression of folate receptors has been identified in specific neoplastic tissues that provides tumor cells with a selective advantage in folic acid uptake and utilization compared to normal tissues. Consequently, this finding suggests the potential utilization of these receptors as targets for selectively inducing photothermal ablation of cancer cells in cancer therapy [43,44].

The simplest and most widely used methods for the synthesis of NCQDs are hydrothermal/solvothermal [15] and microwave irradiation method [3]. Compared to the hydrothermal method, which requires higher reaction temperatures (above 200 °C) and longer reaction times (>1800 s) [45], the microwave irradiation method is faster (<500 s), operates at lower temperatures [46], and can instantly be checked to minimize the risk of overheating of sample [47]. Considering its affordability, simplicity in synthesis conditions, and the availability of easily accessible precursors [3,4], the microwave irradiation method was selected for NCQDs synthesis. PPy nanoparticles are synthesized via a range of methods, including chemical oxidative polymerization [48], microemulsion polymerization [49], electrochemical polymerization [50], template-assisted synthesis [51], and sonochemical synthesis [52]. Among these, chemical oxidative polymerization stands out as the most suitable technique for synthesizing PPy nanoparticles due to its ability for precise control over particle size. This control is achieved by adjusting factors such as the nature and concentration of stabilizer, along with the concentrations of monomer and initiator [53]. In this study, aqueous dispersion polymerization, a form of chemical polymerization, was utilized for PPy synthesis. This method allowed for the regulation of nanoparticle size, aiming to improve its suitability for biological applications at the cellular level. Previously, several efforts have been made to combine PPy nanoparticles with other nanoparticles for concurrent bioimaging and photothermal therapy purposes. Han et al. developed an innovative magnetic therapeutic platform comprising dual photothermal agents, PPy and gold nanoshells, effectively enhancing contrast for imaging and facilitating photothermal therapy [54]. Cao et al. synthesized a folic acid-functionalized gold nanorod@ppy@Fe3O4 nanocomposite for multimodal imaging and chemo-photothermal therapy [55]. Similarly, Wu et al. developed a novel persistent luminescence-PPy nanocomposite for dual-modal imaging-guided photothermal therapy [56]. Gökalp et al. fabricated a ternary nanocomposite of magnetic iron oxide@PPy-nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots, demonstrating photothermal activity with high quantum yield [57]. In these studies, dual photothermal agents combined with contrasting agents were primarily used for imaging and photothermal therapy, mainly relying on X-ray computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), but they lacked fluorescence-based bioimaging. Additionally, there is limited information on the conjugation of biocompatible quantum dots with photothermal agent to fulfill both functions simultaneously, and several studies demonstrated lack of targeting capabilities in nanocomposite. To address these limitations, a distinct biocompatible nanocomposite with a targeting ligand has been designed to achieve higher sensitivity and greater throughput.

This study marks a pioneering effort in synthesizing a targeted theranostic nanocomposite comprising PPy nanoparticles and NCQDs with the capping of folic acid. This innovative composite is designed to harness the distinctive properties of both components, enabling a synergistic combination of bioimaging and photothermal capabilities. The aim is to facilitate the concurrent and targeted detection and destruction of cancer cells, presenting a novel approach in the discipline of tumor theranostics (Fig. 1 ). Moreover, a comprehensive assessment of in vivo and in vitro toxicity was carried out for the nanocomposite, aiming to determine its biocompatibility and organ retention properties. This investigation seeks to broaden the potential applications of the nanocomposite more effectively.

Pyrrole, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(-3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), valine, and folic acid (FA) have been acquired by SRL Chemicals, India. Dimethyl Sulfoxide(DMSO), Iron(III) chloride hexahydrate(FeCl3·6H2O), 1H-pyrrole-1-propionic acid (COOH-Py), polyvinyl alcohol(PVA), and ascorbic acid were purchased from Merck, India. 3-[4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium), trypsin, and fetal bovine albumin (FBS) have been attained from the Himedia Laboratories, India. The source of the antibiotics was Life Technologies (India) Pvt. Ltd. in India. Thermo Fisher Scientific purchased Calcein AM from Invitrogen. The Molecular Probes by Life Technologies, USA provided Annexin V-FITC. MP Biomedicals of USA provided the propidium iodide (PI). Every chemical used in this investigation is of analytical grade. The National Centre for Cell Science in Pune provided the MCF-7 breast cancer cell line and the A549 pulmonary epithelial cell line.

The synthesis of NCQDs was conducted following the microwave-assisted methodology documented earlier by us [3]. Ascorbic acid served as the carbon source, while valine was employed as the nitrogen source in this synthesis.

Carboxylated PPy nanoparticles have been synthesized via aqueous dispersion polymerization approaches involving PVA, a potent oxidizing agent, COOH-Py, and PPy monomers [53,58]. The synthesis parameters were slightly optimized. The process began by dissolving 0.5 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in 15 mL of deionized water through magnetic stirring at 60 °C for 20 min. Following cooling to room temperature, 0.63 g of FeCl3·6H2O was introduced and stirred for an additional 30 min. Subsequently, a mixture of 67 μL of PPy and 59.83 mg of COOH-Py with the molar ratio of 7:3 was dropwise added to the reaction solution after it had cooled to 5 °C. While stirring, the polymerization advanced, causing the mixture solution to turn black within a few minutes. After allowing the polymerization to proceed for 24 h, the resulting black dispersion was washed several times and filtered with 0.45 μm syringe filter to procure final product.

The EDC/NHS conjugation reaction was employed in order to combine carboxylated PPy-NPs with NCQDs. Initially, 1 mL of PPy nanoparticles (1 mg mL−1) in 10 mL of 1 × PBS was mixed and reacted with 2 mM EDC and 5 mM NHS for time period of 25–30 min. Subsequently, 2 mL of NCQDs (3 mg mL−1) was incubated with the solution for 3–4 h. The product was collected utilizing syringe filter. To couple FA with the PPy:NCQDs composite, 10 mg of FA was dissolved in 4 mL of PBS buffer. Subsequently, it was allowed to sit with 50 μL of EDC and NHS (same concentration as above) for 30 min. Following this, 1 mL of PPy:NCQDs composite (2 mg mL−1) was added to this and left for 3–4 h. Then final product was collected by means of a filtration syringe [59].

The optical behavior of nanocomposite was analyzed by observing the distinct absorbance peaks which were inspected utilizing a UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). FT-IR (‘Fourier Transform Infrared’) spectrographic analysis was utilized for characterizing the chemical composition of nanocomposite by identifying the types of functional groups present. The crystalline structural analysis of nanocomposite was conducted using a Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM TECNAI 20G2) operating at 200 kV and an X-ray Diffraction (XRD) measurement employing a Panalytical (Empyrean) diffractometer. The quantum yield of the quantum dots and nanocomposite was determined using the following equation by taking quinine sulphate as a reference [60], based on luminescence spectra obtained with a fluorescence spectroscope (PerkinElmer, USA):

where ɸ is the quantum yield, ɸR is the quantum yield of reference, Asample and AR are the absorbances of sample and reference obtained at 370 nm, ηsample and ηR are the refractive index of the solvent used for sample and reference, and Isample and IR are the intensities of photoluminescence spectra of sample and reference respectively.

MCF-7 cells (FA receptor positive) along with the A549 cells (FA receptor negative) have been cultured in DMEM at the temperature of 37 °C in a humidified incubator with an atmosphere of 5 percent CO2 along with 95 percent air. The DMEM was supplemented with 10 % FBS and 100 mg mL−1 antibiotics. Cell viability has been assessed using the MTT assay, following standard protocol [61]. In brief, MCF-7 cells and A549 cells have been seeded in 96-well plates and allowed to adhere before the addition of varying concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300,400, 500, 600,800 μg ml−1) of PPy:NCQDs: FA nanocomposite. Viability of the cells was assessed through the MTT assay following a 24-h incubation period at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo toxicity measurements of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Upon obtaining agreement “from the ‘Institutional Animal Ethics Committee’ (IAEC) of BHU, Institute of Science, Varanasi (BHU/DoZ/IAEC/2021–2022/001), all in vivo experiments were conducted. Male mice (Swiss albino strain) of six to eight weeks old, weighing approximately 20–25 gm, were accommodated in the animal house of the” department. They were kept under a 12:12 h light and dark photoperiod at consistent temperature. Throughout the study duration, the mice had unrestricted access to standard rodent pellets and water. Subsequently, the 45 mice were divided into three cohorts, each comprising 15 mice. Designated as treatment groups, two of these cohorts underwent dosing, while the third cohort served as the control group. Following a comprehensive literature review, doses of 1 mg kg−1 BW(body weight) and 3 mg kg−1 BW were selected for assessing the subacute toxicity of nanocomposite in mice. Group I received a dose of 1 mg kg−1 BW, Group II received 3 mg kg−1 BW, while the control group was given a 0.9 percent saline solution administered through the tail vein route. The body weight of the mice was consistently monitored throughout the entire 30-day study period. On days one, three, seven, fifteen, and thirty post-dose administration, mice from each group were euthanized, and blood samples were collected for hematological analysis. The kidneys, liver, and spleen were extracted, their weights were recorded, and subsequent homogenates of these organs were prepared for further investigation of various parameters. The protein content was determined using the Bradford technique, employing bovine serum albumin as the standard [62]. Analysis of the crucial antioxidant enzymes, superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase activity, followed the protocols by Das et al. and Claiborne, respectively [63,64]. The assessment of lipid peroxidation was conducted as per the methodology described by Ohkawa et al. [65]. To investigate tissue necrosis or potential pathological conditions associated with nanocomposite dosage at the tissue level, slides of the kidney, liver, and spleen were prepared and stained with Hematoxylin & Eosin and subjected to examination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal effect measurements of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The photothermal effect of PPy:NCQDs: FA nanocomposite was assessed by monitoring the temperature variation of a solution containing nanocomposite during laser irradiation at 808 nm. For this, 1 mL of varying concentrations (30, 50, 100, 500, 1000 μg mL−1) of both the PPy nanoparticles and nanocomposite suspension was placed into a 1 mL quartz cuvette. Subsequently, the suspensions were allowed to undergo irradiation with an 808 nm NIR laser at a power density of 1Wcm−2 for a duration of 10 min. Temperature measurements were recorded at 1-min time intervals throughout the irradiation period and a thermal profile was displayed. Another crucial aspect of photothermal therapy involves determining the photothermal conversion efficiency of the photothermal agent. To evaluate the photothermal conversion efficiency, the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite (1000 μg mL−1) was continuously irradiated with an NIR laser (1 Wcm−2, 808 nm) for 10 min, followed by natural cooling to room temperature. To assess photothermal stability, the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite (1000 μg mL−1) underwent 5 cycles of NIR laser irradiation (10 min laser on at 1 W cm−2, 10 min laser off), with the solution temperature being measured throughout the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted cell imaging and photothermal ablation of cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating potential application of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite, MCF-7 (FA+) and A549 (FA−) cells have been seeded in 96-well plates (for MTT analysis) as well as in 24-well plates (for Calcein AM/PI and Annexin V/PI assessment) at an initial density of 1 × 105 cells. The plates were then shifted in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C, allowing the cells to adhere for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells of 96-well plates were treated with the nanocomposite at varying concentrations of 100,200, 300, 400,500 μg mL−1 for a 4-h incubation period, followed by exposure to 808 nm laser at a power density of 1Wcm−2 for different time periods as 1 min, 3 min and 5 min. The cells in the 24-well plates were treated with 100 μg mL−1 of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite after selecting the ideal dose from the MTT experiment. Subsequently, the laser irradiation procedure was repeated, followed by the Calcein AM/PI and Annexin V/PI assays.

Preliminary observation of photothermal killing ability of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite in both MCF-7 and A549 cells was conducted by considering different concentrations of nanocomposite using the standard MTT assay [61].

Cell viability has been analyzed through Calcein AM/PI staining [66]. After the laser irradiation, the culture media were removed from the plates and the cells have been washed by using 1x PBS. A mixture of 150 μl Calcein AM (5 μM) and 150 μl PI (5 μM) has been then added to the cells, followed by a 15–20 min incubation in darkness at 37 °C. Post-incubation, the staining solution has been removed, and cells underwent two washes by using 1x PBS. Subsequently, visualization was performed using a ‘Confocal Laser Scanning Super Resolution Microscope’ (SP8 STED; Leica Microsystems). For investigating the imaging properties of NCQDs in the nanocomposite, specific groups of wells were exclusively stained with PI only, while the subsequent procedures of staining remained consistent.

In addition to Calcein AM/PI staining, Annexin V/PI staining has been employed to investigate apoptosis induced by laser irradiation. The fraction of apoptotic cells was determined by assessing phosphatidylserine (PS) translocation and plasma membrane integrity [67]. Following the laser irradiation procedure, 1 × 105 cells have been harvested via centrifugation and resuspended in 100 μl of 1x PBS. Subsequently, 2 μl of Annexin V-FITC was introduced to suspension and left to incubate for 15 min in the dark. Prior to flow cytometric analysis, 2 μl of PI was added, and then the tubes were subsequently subjected to analysis.

The results were validated by conducting the experiments three times, and the mean ± SD was used to express the results. The GraphPad Prism 10.1.0 software was utilized to conduct statistical analysis, utilizing the paired t-test along with the one-way ANOVA. P-values<0.05 have been taken into consideration for statistical significance.

The UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra have been utilized to showcase optical properties of the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite. Along with the individual peaks of PPy nanoparticles (blue), NCQDs (red), and FA (black), the UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra for nanocomposite (green) were presented in Fig. 2 (a), revealing three absorption bands at approximately 282 nm, 351 nm, and 438 nm. These align with the absorption wavelengths characteristic of FA, NCQDs, and PPy nanoparticles respectively [68–70]. Additionally, the nanocomposite exhibited significant near-infrared (NIR) absorption spanning 650–900 nm, implying enhanced photothermal conversion efficiency and the potential for photothermal treatment capability. Fig. 2 (b) displayed the FT-IR absorption spectra of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite (green curve), PPy nanoparticles (blue curve), NCQDs (red curve), and FA (black curve). The prominent peaks at 1480 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 of the composite demonstrated the presence of CN group vibration which corresponds to the individual peaks of the PPy (1472 cm−1), NCQDs (1400 cm−1), and FA (1402 cm−1). The transmittance peaks of nanocomposite attributed to CH (alkyl) group vibrations were clearly shown at 2920 cm−1, 1192 cm−1 and 910 cm−1 which are in accordance with alkyl groups representing peaks of individual nanoparticles. The peak of nanocomposite at 1040 cm−1 has been allotted to the stretching molecular vibration of -C-O-C group which is aligned with the peak of the -C-O-C group vibration of NCQDs at 1045 cm−1. The characteristic peak of NH and O–H stretching vibrations at 3260 cm−1 represented by NCQDs corresponds to the 3280 cm−1 of the composite. Significantly, the transmittance peaks of the composite at 2920 cm−1, 1685 cm−1,and 1400 cm−1, presenting the –CONH– groups, indicated the successful capping of folic acid onto the composite [59,71,72].

The HRTEM pictures in Fig. 3 (a) validate the creation of finely isolated nanosized (3.131 ± 1.02 nm) spherical shaped NCQDs. The depiction of single N-CQD unveils distinct lattice fringes with a spacing of approximately 0.22 nm. This lattice spacing aligns with the (110) diffraction patterns of graphite facets, suggesting a structural resemblance to graphite. The obtained PPy nanoparticles also exhibit a spherical to oval morphology, although their size is significantly larger (31.508 ± 8.615 nm) as shown in Fig. 3 (b). The PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite demonstrated notably larger agglomerates, measuring approximately 43.504 ± 9.585 nm in size as represented in Fig. 3 (c). The presence of FA on composite is clearly identified in Fig. 3 (d), affirming the presence of ligand on nanocomposite for folate receptors, demonstrating potential clinical utility of composite. In Fig. 3 (e), the XRD pattern of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite, PPy nanoparticles, N-CQDs and FA is showcased. The pattern displays a distinct diffraction peak at approximately 25.12° and another diffraction peak at 11.2° in the composite (green curve). These observations indicate the existence of an amorphous carbon phase of PPy nanoparticles as well as NCQDs and semi-crystalline phase of FA in the nanocomposite, further demonstrating the successful conjugation of PPy nanoparticles with NCQDs and FA [3,73,74].

The determination of quantum yield and photostability of quantum dots are crucial for optimizing their performance in bioimaging applications. High quantum yield indicates efficient photon emission, which is essential for bright fluorescence, while photostability ensures that this efficient emission is maintained over time without significant degradation, providing consistent performance in imaging applications [60,75,76]. In this work, the quantum yield of NCQDs was determined to be 36.01 ± 0.44 %, with quinine sulphate serving as the reference compound (with a quantum yield of 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4 at 370 nm). The quantum yield of the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite was independently calculated to be 36.76 ± 0.33 %. The slight alteration in the quantum yield of the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite can be explained by the similar π-conjugated structure of PPy and NCQDs, with the nitrogen heteroatom on the pyrrole ring as well as on NCQDs potentially causing a doping effect. This interaction may tune the luminescence, leading to the creation of uncommon surface states [77,78]. Moreover, the photostability of NCQDs and PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite in aqueous solution was examined under continuous irradiation (λex = 370 nm). The NCQDs and PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite exhibited a decrease in fluorescence intensity of only 5.25 ± 0.09 % and 4.99 ± 0.08 % respectively over a period of 60 min, demonstrating remarkable resistance to photobleaching (Figs. S5a and b in supporting information). The marginal change in quantum yield and photostability of the nanocomposite suggests that it exhibits improved performance when synergistically fabricated into nanocomposite form.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The consideration of the biocompatibility of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite is crucial when investigating nanoparticles for potential biological applications. In this study, an in vitro investigation was carried out using MCF-7 (FA+) and A549 (FA−) cells, exposing them to different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 800 μg mL-1) of nanocomposite for a duration of 24 h. The cell viability at different concentrations of nanocomposite was assessed using the MTT test. No significant cytotoxic effects were observed in both cell types at concentrations below 600 μg mL−1 for nanocomposite (Fig. 4 ). Only when exposed to a relatively high concentration (800 μg mL−1) did a slight reduction in the viabilities of MCF-7 as well as A549 cells, with approximately 70–75 % viability compared to the untreated control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of in vivo toxicity of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The supplementary information section of this article provides details on the in vivo toxicity of the nanocomposite.

Temperature profile evaluation stands out as a key characteristic of an effective photothermal agent. Photothermal performance of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite was investigated in an aqueous solution with concentrations spanning from 30 to 1000 μg mL−1 under NIR illumination (808 nm laser, power density: 1Wcm−2). Upon irradiation, the nanocomposite exhibited a rapid increase in temperature, reaching 56–57 °C after 5 min of NIR exposure at concentration of 1000 μg mL−1, which is displayed in Fig. 5 . To validate the efficacy of PPy, a proficient photothermal agent [79], in its nanocomposite configuration, the temperature profile of PPy nanoparticles was scrutinized individually and compared with that of the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite. The examination demonstrated that the photothermal performance of PPy nanoparticles slightly increased by approximately <1 °C when incorporated into a nanocomposite. This indicates a synergistic effect achieved after synthesizing the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite. The slight temperature increase suggests enhanced performance which may be due to the interaction between the components in the nanocomposite. Another important aspect of this study is that even after prolonged laser exposure of 10 min, PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite demonstrated exceptional temperature consistency, confirming that the synthesized nanocomposite is an excellent photothermal agent for PTT.

The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of the synthesized nanocomposite was determined according to the method described in the literature [80–82]:
η =

Here, ℎ represents the heat transfer coefficient, S is the surface area of cuvette, and Q
Dis denotes the heat dissipated due to the light absorbed by the solvent and cuvette, I refers to the laser power used in the photothermal experiment, while A
808 is the absorbance at 808 nm. T max and T
Surr indicate the maximum temperature of the sample and the ambient room temperature, respectively. By utilizing the above equation, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite was calculated and found to be 41.28 ± 1.36 % (Fig. 6 (a) and b). The detailed calculations are provided in section S.3.4of the supplementary information. In comparison to other reported polypyrrole nanoparticles for photothermal therapy [59,83], the synthesized nanocomposite in this research demonstrated a commendable photothermal conversion efficiency.

To verify the recyclability of the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite under NIR light, five cycles of laser on/off were performed. It was found out that the maximum and minimum temperatures of nanocomposite remained nearly constant (Figure 6c). This outcome demonstrated outstanding heat reproducibility and photothermal stability of the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted cell imaging and photothermal effect examination
ce:section.ce:para: MTT test, Calcein AM/PI double staining, and Annexin V-FITC/PI staining are the three commonly used methods to assess cell viability and apoptosis. The MTT examination aided in determining the optimal concentration of nanocomposite for advancing further. Staining with Calcein AM and PI demonstrated cell viability by detecting live cells in green and dead cells in red fluorescence. The combination of these two dyes allows for the differentiation of live and dead cells under a microscope. By using Annexin V-FITC/PI staining, one can differentiate between early apoptotic, late apoptotic, necrotic, and viable cells, providing valuable information about the cell death pathways induced as a result of photothermal therapy [67,84].

The data presented in Fig. 7 (a) illustrates a significant reduction in MCF-7 cell viability to 30.27 ± 0.068 % when exposed to a concentration of 100 μg mL−1 PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite and irradiated with NIR laser for 5 min. In comparison, MCF-7 cells treated with 100 μg mL−1 PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite and irradiated with 3 min of laser, exhibited 67.65 ± 1.39 % cell survival. Interestingly, the application of the same concentration of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite did not impact the viability of MCF-7 cells, with over 90.81267 ± 0.31 % cell survival observed after 1 min of laser exposure. The presence of folic acid receptors on MCF-7 cells likely facilitated the binding of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite to the MCF-7 cells, thereby increasing the temperature and leading to the killing of cancer cells [85,86]. In Fig. 7 (b), the viability of A549 cells treated with 100 μg mL−1 of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite was determined to be 96.28 ± 0.38 %, 92.45 ± 0.45 %, and 90.75 ± 0.26 %, after 1 min, 3 min and 5 min of laser irradiation respectively. The possible reason for this could be the absence of folic acid receptors on A549 cells [85,87,88], preventing the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite from binding to them. Consequently, even after laser exposure, the temperature does not elevate sufficiently to kill the cancer cells.

This study suggests a varying impact of the dose and time period of laser on the viability of MCF-7 and A549 cells, culminating in the selection of 100 μg mL−1 dose of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite as an ideal dose and 5 min of irradiation as an ideal time period for this therapy. Thus, selective ablation of folate receptor positive cancer cells was successfully achieved using PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite, displaying their exceptional targeting capability and photothermal therapy proficiency.

Fig. 8 illustrates the well-picturized findings of the Calcein AM /PI double staining. Three groups were established i.e., Group A, Group B and Group C to assess targeted photothermal therapy on MCF-7 (FA+) and A549 (FA−) cells using the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite. Initially, all groups were provided with treatment of 100 μg mL−1 of the nanocomposite except the control subgroup. Following exposure to a near-infrared (NIR) laser at 1 Wcm−2 for 5 min, MCF-7 cells in Group A, incubated with 100 μg mL−1 of nanocomposite, experienced considerable destruction (Fig. 8 (d)). In contrast, the control group shown in Fig. 8 (a), consisting of cells without nanocomposite incubation, remained unaffected even after a 5-min laser exposure. In similar way, after 1 min of laser exposure, the MCF-7 cells showed minimal impact, whereas moderate cell death was observed after 3 min of irradiation in Group A as shown in Fig. 8 (b) and (c) respectively. In Group B, MCF-7 cells underwent treatment with the same dose of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite. Following NIR irradiation, the cells underwent staining with PI exclusively for the identification of dead cells, thus allowing for the evaluation of the composite's imaging properties by distinguishing live cells. Here, it is evident that the control subgroup, treated solely with NCQDs for cell imaging, exhibited no discernible effects under laser irradiation (Fig. 8 (e)). Conversely, the remaining subgroups within Group B demonstrated negligible impact after 1 min of irradiation, a slightly noticeable effect after 3 min, and a substantial increase in cell death with nearly all cells affected after 5 min of irradiation (Fig. 8(f) (g) (h)). These demonstrations unequivocally establish the imaging and photothermal properties of the synthesized composite. To validate the targeting capability of the synthesized composite, A549 cells, which are folic acid receptor negative [89], were employed and presented in Group C of this work. In the control subgroup of this group, where no dose of the composite was administered, laser irradiation for 5 min was applied. The observation revealed an absence of cell death, as illustrated in Fig. 8(i). Even with the administration of the nanocomposite dose in the remaining subgroups, no dead cells were observed after 1, 3, and 5 min of NIR irradiation (Fig. 8 (j), (k), (l)). This suggests that the synthesized nanocomposite specifically binds to cells with folate receptors, confirming the successful synthesis of a targeted photothermal agent with imaging capabilities.

To validate the above conclusions, Annexin V/PI staining was performed on MCF-7 cells. By facilitating the identification of viable, early apoptotic, late apoptotic, and necrotic cell percentages, the results from this study affirmed the excellent photothermal activity of the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite. For the control group without nanocomposite dosage and subjected to 5 min of NIR irradiation, the percentage of viable cells was determined to be 99.78 ± 0.046 %, early apoptotic cells were 0.0096 ± 0.015 %, late apoptotic cells were 0.033 ± 0.058 %, and necrotic cells were 0.09 ± 0.02 %. After 1 min of laser irradiation, the percentage of cells determined were: viable cells 85.8 ± 0.46 %, early apoptotic cells 1.37 ± 0.065 %, late apoptotic cells 10.61 ± 0.37 % and necrotic cells 2.22 ± 0.55 %. Following 3 min of laser irradiation, the determined percentages of cells were viable cells 56.22 ± 0.25 %, early apoptotic cells 2.12 ± 0.08 %, late apoptotic cells 31.37 ± 0.13 %, and necrotic cells 10.29 ± 0.41 %. Finally, after 5 min of laser treatment, the cell percentages changed drastically, viable cells 19.64 ± 0.38 %, early apoptotic cells 1.14 ± 0.07 %, late apoptotic cells 16.65 ± 0.28 %, and necrotic cells 62.57 ± 0.18 %. As compared to control, there was a significant increase in the fractions of early apoptotic, late apoptotic, and necrotic cells (p < 0.05) after the treatment of MCF-7 cells with NIR irradiation for 5 min (Fig. 9 a-e). This action of nanocomposite can be attributed to the precise conjugation of folic acid on the folate receptors present on folate receptor-positive cells. This specific interaction ensures the localization of polypyrrole nanoparticles onto cancer cells. The extended electron cloud resonance property of polypyrrole, resulting from the delocalization of π electrons, allows it to efficiently absorb substantial light in the NIR region. This absorption leads to hyperthermia within cancer cells. The elevated temperature induces the destruction of the mitochondrial membrane, triggering factors associated with cell apoptosis, such as the release of cytochrome c, ultimately leading to cell death [35,37]. In addition to apoptosis, necrosis is a common cellular response to photothermal cancer treatment, generally influenced by laser power and exposure time. High energy irradiation typically induces necrosis, while low energy irradiation promotes apoptosis [90]. In this study, with the laser power fixed at 1 W cm−2, exposure time primarily dictates cell necrosis. As exposure time increases to 5 min, the resulting high temperature causes cell swelling and membrane rupture, leading to the release of intracellular constituents into the extracellular environment. This triggers inflammatory responses, culminating in necrosis [91].

In summary, this study resulted in the synthesis of a theranostic PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite with the capability for specific bioimaging and PTT. Upon excitation at 488 nm, the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite demonstrated bright green fluorescence and exhibit excellent photostability, enabling precise monitoring of only FA-positive cancer cells through the luminescence signal. Furthermore, even at a higher concentration of up to 600 μg mL−1 in cancer cells, the PPy:NCQDs: FA nanocomposite exhibits low cytotoxicity. A study on the in vivo toxicity of PPy:NCQDs: FA nanocomposite additionally showed that there was no observed acute toxicity with experimental dosages of 1 & 3 mg per kg body weight. The in vitro research results validate the in vivo research findings, affirming the suitability of these dots for biomedical theranostics. Upon exposure to an NIR laser for 5 min, the PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite, characterized by a high photostability, demonstrated a considerable rise in temperature. This unique property enables PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite to effectively induce cancer cell death. The remarkable PTT capability of PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite is distinctly evident through both their inherent green fluorescence and a live/dead cell assay through Calcein AM/PI staining. Furthermore, detailed analysis of the apoptosis induced by PTT is conducted to further verify the fabrication of nanocomposite. Consequently, PPy:NCQDs:FA nanocomposite, a theranostic platform with stable photoluminescence, high photostability, low cytotoxicity, and tumor-targeting properties, enable optical detection and selective mortality of FA positive cancer cells.
